citation.title: Tailored l -Arginine modified Poly(piperazine-amide) nanofiltration membrane with enhanced water permeability for efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation

content: A B S T R A C T In recent years, there has been a growing focus on lithium recovery from water resources using thin-film composite (TFC) nanofiltration (NF) membranes. In this study, a poly(piperazine-amide) NF membrane was fabricated via interfacial polymerization (IP) between piperazine (PIP) and trimesoyl chloride (TMC) to effec­tively separate Li+ from Mg2+. However, the trade-off between water permeability and Li+/Mg2+ selectivity presented a challenge, requiring additional surface modification to optimize performance. The surface of the poly (PIP-amide) membrane was modified with ARG amine-based hydrophilic monomer, followed by crosslinking with glutaraldehyde (GLA). The amine groups of ARG are expected to interact with the unreacted acyl chloride groups of TMC, increasing the positive surface charge and thereby improving Li+/Mg2+ selectivity. The mem­brane modified with ARG and GLA (A2-G0.3) exhibited a significantly improved Li+/Mg2+ selectivity of 17.11, compared to 5.14 for the unmodified membrane when tested in a solution containing 2000 ppm of salts (Li+/ Mg2+ of 1:20). Notably, the A2-G0.3 membrane demonstrated a Li+ rejection of -45.4 and Mg2+ rejection of 91.5 %, with a water flux of 47.0 Lm-2h-1 at 70 psi. When tested with a simulated brine with a total salt concentration exceeding 21,000 ppm, the membrane exhibited a Li+ rejection of 9.5 % and Mg2+ rejection of 90.1 %, along with a water flux of 18.6 Lm-2h-1 at low pressure of 70 psi.

The membrane maintained consistent performance over 200 h of simulated feed filtration, demonstrating its long-term stability. Moreover, the anti­fouling performance of the membrane was greatly improved by grafting the ARG-GLA layer onto its surface. These findings highlight the modified membrane’s potential for effective lithium recovery in high-salinity environments.

content: 1. Introduction Lithium, as an alkali metal, is known for its strong electrochemical activity, with a standard electrode potential of -3.05 V. It also possesses the highest specific heat capacity (3.6 J/g.K) among solid elements, making it crucial for energy storage applications [1]. In recent years, the demand for lithium has grown substantially, driven by the ongoing development of portable electronics and electric vehicles [2]. Currently, the primary sources of lithium are brines and lithium ores, with global reserves collectively around 34 million tons [3]. Although lithium ores can contain higher amounts of lithium, their extraction processes require significant energy and water consumption, diminishing their economic viability [4]. In contrast, lithium recovery from salt-lake brines offers a more environmentally friendly and cost-effective approach [5]. However, the primary challenge in Li+ recovery from brines is the presence of various other cations, including Na+, K+, Mg2+, and Ca2+. Notably, Mg2+ and Li + have similar chemical properties and close hydrated diameters (Mg2+: 0.856 nm, Li+: 0.764 nm), making their separation difficult [2]. Extensive research has explored lithium recovery from brines using methods such as solar evaporation, precipitation, solvent extraction, direct lithium extraction (DLE), electrochemical techniques, and mem­brane separation [6]. Among these methods, nanofiltration (NF) mem­branes stand out for their high Li+/Mg2+selectivity, minimal

The separation of Li+ and Mg2+ with NF membranes is governed by several mechanisms, including steric hindrance, dielectric exclusion, and Donnan exclusion. These mechanisms allow Li + to permeate the membrane while effec­tively rejecting Mg2+ [7]. Li+, with a smaller hydrated diameter (0.764 nm) compared to Mg2+ (0.856 nm), experiences less steric hindrance, facilitating its passage through the membrane [8]. Moreover, the sig­nificant reduction in the dielectric constant of water within nanopores, compared to bulk water, affects ion solvation, enhancing ion selectivity [9]. Ions must partially shed their hydration layer to traverse nanopores, a process known as dehydration [3]. Li+, with a lower dehydration en­ergy (-515 kJ mol-1) compared to Mg2+ (-1828 kJ mol-1), encounters a smaller energy barrier when shedding its hydration shell to enter and pass through the nanopores [10]. Lastly, electrostatic interactions be­tween ions and the intrinsic charges on the membrane surface can in­fluence ion selectivity. Based on the Donnan exclusion theory, Mg2+, with a higher positive charge than Li+, faces stronger repulsion from membranes with positively charged surfaces [11].

content: Donnan exclusion theory, Mg2+, with a higher positive charge than Li+, faces stronger repulsion from membranes with positively charged surfaces [11]. Commercial NF membranes, such as NF 90, NF 270, and Desal-5, possess inherent negative charges due to the presence of carboxyl groups on their surfaces, resulting in relatively low Li+/Mg2+ selectivity [12]. Consequently, increasing attention has been directed towards developing positively charged thin-film composite (TFC) nanofiltration membranes using interfacial polymerization (IP). This technique in­volves the polymerization of two immiscible monomers at the interface between an organic and an aqueous phase to form a selective layer on a porous substrate [13]. Most studies on positively charged NF mem­branes have primarily utilized polyethyleneimine (PEI), a monomer with a high amine density, in the aqueous phase [14], or applied PEI as a surface coating on negatively charged NF membranes [15]. However, the large molecular size and uneven stacking of PEI result in non-uniform polymerization on the membrane surface. Furthermore, the linear aliphatic structure of PEI intensifies crosslinking during the IP reaction, reducing the available free volume and significantly dimin­ishing water permeability [16]. To overcome these limitations, recent studies have used piperazine (PIP) in the aqueous phase to fabricate lithium-selective TFC membranes. For example, Lu et al.

content: these limitations, recent studies have used piperazine (PIP) in the aqueous phase to fabricate lithium-selective TFC membranes. For example, Lu et al. [17] developed a poly(PIP-amide) membrane that achieved approximately 55 % rejec­tion for MgCl2 and 15 % rejection for LiCl, with a water permeability of 6.5 Lm-2h-1bar-1. Similarly, Wu et al. [18] reported LiCl and MgCl2 rejections of 12.4 % and 39.6 %, respectively, with the water perme­ability of 7.0 Lm-2h-1bar-1 for their NF membrane developed with PIP. These findings indicate that poly(PIP-amide) NF membranes fabricated with conventional IP technique offer limited Li+/Mg2+ selectivity, pri­marily due to their highly negatively charged surface [19]. A recent study reports the fabrication of PA NF membranes with a Mg2+ rejection rate exceeding 99.9 %, achieved by modifying the IP process using an oil-soluble surfactant, dodecyl phosphate (DDP). This surfactant forms a monolayer at the water/oil interface, effectively regulating the mem­brane pores within the range of 3.2–8.0 Å. However, the low water permeability of approximately 7 Lm-2h-1bar-1 poses a significant challenge for practical applications [20]. Therefore, developing a practical approach for modifying the surface properties of poly (PIP-amide) NF membranes is essential for Li+/Mg2+ separation while enhancing water permeability.

content: pproach for modifying the surface properties of poly (PIP-amide) NF membranes is essential for Li+/Mg2+ separation while enhancing water permeability. Surface modification through chemical grafting has emerged as a promising method, wherein an appropriate monomer is applied to the surface of the TFC membrane [21]. Typically, the monomer contains multiple amine functional groups that react with residual acyl chloride groups on the membrane surface, imparting a more positive charge [22]. So far, different salts containing quaternary ammonium or imidazolium groups such as 1-(3-amino­propyl)-3-methylimidazolium bis(trifluoromethane sulfonyl)imide [18], (3,5-dimethyl hydrazide-benzyl trimethyl ammonium bromide [11], quaternized bipyridine bromide [2], 2,3-epoxypropyl trimethyl ammonium chloride [23], and quaternized tetra hydroxyethyl imida­zolium [24] have been used to increase the positive charge of the membrane surface. However, synthesizing these modifiers is costly and time-consuming, restricting their applications. Therefore, there is a need for a more accessible, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly modifier to enhance the surface properties of lithium-selective membranes. L-arginine (ARG) is a biomaterial with hydrophilic components, such as a guanidine group, an α-amino group, and an α-carboxylic acid group, playing a vital role in the construction of aquaporins (AQPs) in the human body [25].

content: anidine group, an α-amino group, and an α-carboxylic acid group, playing a vital role in the construction of aquaporins (AQPs) in the human body [25]. AQPs are transmembrane proteins with an hourglass-like inner structure, serving as water channels. Specifically, ARG contributes to the formation of the narrowest segment of AQP channels and facilitates the rapid transport of water through hydrogen bonding with water molecules [26]. Therefore, in this study, we employed ARG to modify the selective layer of poly(piperazine-amide) TFC membranes, leveraging ARG’s hy­drophilic nature and positive charge to improve the water permeability and Li+/Mg2+ separation. The active amine groups of ARG were designed to react with the residual acyl chloride groups of TMC, increasing the positive surface charge of the membrane. However, due to the partial hydrolysis of acyl chlorides during the interfacial poly­merization process and exposure to ambient moisture, the number of available acyl chloride groups for ARG attachment was limited [27]. To overcome this challenge, we introduced glutaraldehyde (GLA) as a chemical crosslinker. The aldehyde groups of GLA facilitated the cova­lent bonding between the amine groups of both PIP and ARG, allowing further attachment of ARG to the membrane surface and ensuring a more uniform modification [28].

Experimental methods 2.1. Materials A commercial polyether sulfone (PES) ultrafiltration (UF) mem­brane, supplied by GreEnvi Corp. (Edmonton, Canada), was utilized as the substrate for fabricating the TFC NF membranes. Piperazine (PIP, ≥99 %), trimesoyl chloride (TMC, 98 %), n-Hexane (≥99 %), and glutaraldehyde (GLA, 50 wt% in water) were sourced from Sigma Aldrich. L-arginine monohydrochloride (ARG, ≥98 %) was purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific to modify the membrane surface. Poly­ethylene glycol (PEG) with different molecular weights (200, 400, 600, and 1000 g mol-1) purchased from Sigma Aldrich were used to deter­mine the molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of the membranes. The salts used to evaluate the membrane performance, including lithium chloride (LiCl, ≥99 %), sodium chloride (NaCl, ≥99 %), potassium chloride (KCl, ≥99 %), magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl2.6H20, ≥99 %), cal­cium chloride (CaCl2, ≥99 %) sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, ≥99 %), and magnesium sulfate (MgSO4, ≥99 %) were all purchased from Fisher Scientific.

Fabrication of TFC NF membranes The TFC NF membranes were fabricated through the IP process using different concentrations of PIP in an aqueous solution and TMC in an organic solution onto a PES substrate. The initial step involved dis­solving PIP with various concentrations (0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, and 3 wt%) in DI water. The resulting aqueous solution was then applied to the PES substrate, allowing it to rest for 2 min to ensure complete saturation. The excess solution was subsequently removed by employing an acrylic roller. Next, the TMC-n-hexane solution (0.15 wt%) was poured over the

Surface modification of TFC membranes To modify the membrane surface, the P0.5 sample was selected and immersed in aqueous ARG solutions at varying concentrations of 0.5, 1, 2, and 3 wt%. The modified membranes were then heat-treated in an oven at 80 ◦C for 5 min. Finally, the crosslinked membranes were washed with DI water to remove any unreacted chemicals from the surface. Antifouling performance of membranes The antifouling performance of the P0.5 and A2-G0.3 membranes was assessed using three different foulant solutions: (a) 250 ppm SA with 0.5 mM CaCl2, (b) 250 ppm HA, and (c) 250 ppm BSA. The experiments were carried out in a cross-flow filtration system at 25 ◦C and 70 psi. Before initiating the fouling tests, each membrane underwent a 30-min compaction phase, during which the initial pure water flux (Jo) was measured. The feed solution was then replaced with the foulant solution, and the fouling test proceeded for 8 h, with continuous monitoring of the water flux (J1). After fouling, the membranes were cleaned with pure water at a high flow rate for 30 min to remove residual foulants. Following the cleaning step, the pure water flux (Jwf) was recorded for another 30 min. To further investigate the antifouling performance of the membranes, this 8.5-h cycle was repeated for a second round. content: 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Chemical composition of the membranes The TFC membranes were fabricated through the IP process, where the amine groups of PIP reacted with the acyl chloride groups of TMC, forming a PA layer on the substrate. The hydrolysis of any unreacted acyl chlorides of TMC forms carboxyl groups, which deprotonate and impart a negative charge to the membrane surface [31]. ARG was introduced to further modify the membrane surface, where its amine groups reacted with the remaining unreacted acyl chlorides, followed by crosslinking with GLA. ARG can attach to the membrane surface by forming a Schiff base between the unreacted aldehyde groups of the grafted GLA and the primary amine group of ARG, creating an additional functional layer on the PA surface [28], as illustrated in Fig. 1. The chemical compositions of the substrate, unmodified P0.5 mem­brane, and surface-coated A2-G0.3 membrane were analyzed using ATRFTIR, as illustrated in Fig. 2a. The peak at 1654 cm-1 in both P0.5 and A2-G0.3 membranes corresponds to the C––O stretch of amide groups in the crosslinked PA layer, indicating the successful polymerization be­tween the amine groups of PIP or ARG and acyl chloride groups of TMC [32]. Moreover, a peak at 1681 cm-1 appears in the A2-G0.3 membrane, which is absent in the P0.5 membrane. This peak is attributed to imine groups (C––N), confirming the successful surface modification of the membrane with ARG and GLA [33].

However, other specific charac­teristic peaks of ARG and GLA were not observed, potentially obscured by the strong peaks of the PES substrate and PA layer [34,35]. XPS analysis was further employed to investigate the surface composition of the P0.5 and A2-G0.3 membranes. The survey spectra identified C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s peaks for both membranes (Fig. 2b and Table 2). The atomic concentration of O increased from 20.02 % in the P0.5 membrane to 45.63 % in the A2-G0.3 membrane, confirming the successful formation of an ARG-GLA grafting layer with higher O con­tent on the PA surface. Additionally, high-resolution C 1s, N 1s and O 1s XPS spectra were used to elucidate the composition of functional groups in P0.5 and A2-G0.3 membranes (Table 2 and Fig. 2c–e). The C 1s region of the P0.5 membrane can be deconvoluted into five distinct peaks, including C-C/C-H at 285.0 eV, C-COOH/C-CONH at 285.7 eV, C-NH-at 286.3 eV, N-C––O at 288.1 eV, and O-C––O at 289.0 eV [36]. In the A2-G0.3 membrane, a new peak at 287.7 eV indicates the presence of C––N bonds with a relative concentration of 6.01 %, further supporting the formation of the ARG-GLA grafting layer [37]. Additionally, the reaction of PIP and TMC at the same concentration yields nearly iden­tical C-COO/C-CON concentrations in both the P0.5 and A2-G0.3 membranes.

The AFM results indicate that the A2-G0.3 has a surface average roughness (Ra) of 11.1 nm, making it slightly rougher than P0.5, which has a Ra of 8.7 nm. The amine groups in the ARG structure reacted either with residual acyl chlorides on the PA layer or with unreacted aldehyde groups of the grafted GLA, imparting multiple extension directions to the membrane surface and thereby increasing its roughness [35]. The cross-sectional morphologies of both P0.5 and A2-G0.3 mem­branes were further investigated using TEM and SEM images, as shown in Fig. 3c and d. Both membranes show a typical asymmetric structure with an ultrathin PA layer on the porous PES substrate. However, analysis of TEM images (Fig. 3c) revealed that the thickness of the se­lective layer increased from approximately 39 nm in the P0.5 membrane to 47 nm in the A2-G0.3 membrane. This slight increase in thickness is attributed to the ARG-GLA grafting layer on the membrane surface. The cross-sectional SEM images (Fig. 3d) further confirm this observation, showing an increase in the selective layer thickness from approximately 40 nm in the P0.5 membrane to 48 nm in the A2-G0.3 membrane.

content: 3.3. Hydrophilicity, surface charge, and pore size of the membranes Surface hydrophilicity plays a crucial role in determining the per­formance of NF membranes, as a higher affinity for water molecules enhances water permeability. To assess this property, the surface wettability of the P0.5 and A2-G0.3 membranes was evaluated by measuring their WCA (Fig. 4a). The P0.5 membrane demonstrated a hydrophilic surface, with a WCA of 43.9o ± 2.6, which is due to the presence of carboxyl groups on the membrane surface created through the hydrolysis of the unreacted acyl chlorides of TMC. The formation of an ARG-GLA grafting layer on the surface of the A2-G0.3 membrane further decreased the WCA to 34.1◦± 3.1. ARG contains abundant amine groups, which not only reacted with the residual acyl chlorides of TMC but also introduced additional hydrophilic groups onto the mem­brane surface [35]. Furthermore, the guanidine group of ARG exists as a guanidinium cation (Gdm+) in the aqueous solution, which can form stable Gdm+–Gdm+ pairs despite the electrostatic repulsion [34]. This pairing is stabilized by combining cavitation effects, quad­rupole–quadrupole interactions, and dispersion forces. These Gdm+–Gdm+ pairs bind with water molecules through hydrogen bonding, creating a hydration layer on the membrane surface that further improves its hydrophilicity [42].

Moreover, the rougher surface morphology of the A2-G0.3 membrane, as described by the Wenzel equation, provided a larger surface area for water contact, further improving its wettability [43]. The surface charge of the P0.5 and A2-G0.3 membranes was evalu­ated through zeta potential measurements, as depicted in Fig. 4b. The A2-G0.3 membrane exhibited a more positive surface charge than the P0.5 membrane, as the amine groups of ARG reacted with the residual acyl chlorides, preventing their hydrolysis into carboxyl groups. Notably, each ARG molecule carries a net positive charge, as the ratio of amine groups is higher than that of carboxyl groups. In the aqueous environment, these amine groups protonate, further enhancing the membrane’s positive charge. Additionally, at pH values below 12.1, guanidinium groups primarily exist as Gdm+ cations, further contrib­uting to the increase in positive surface charge [35]. The pore size of lithium-selective membranes is a crucial factor in achieving high water flux and Li+/Mg2+ selectivity. As shown in Fig. 4c, the MWCO of the P0.5 and A2-G0.3 membranes were 277 and 288 Da, respectively. The nearly identical MWCO values indicate that surface modification has minimal impact on pore structure.

Fig. 7b shows the effect of pH on the performance of A2-G0.3 membrane in mixed salt solutions (2000 ppm total concentration, Li+/ Mg2+ ratio of 1:20). The membrane’s ion rejection behavior is strongly governed by the protonation and deprotonation of surface functional groups, particularly carboxyl (-COOH) and amine (-NH2) groups, which influence the membrane’s surface charge and its electrostatic in­teractions with ions in the feed solution. Under acidic conditions (pH 4), the amine groups are protonated (-NH3+), while carboxyl groups remain mostly undissociated (-COOH), resulting in a highly positively charged membrane surface. This strong positive charge increased the electro­static repulsion of divalent cation, leading to a high Mg2+ rejection of 99.13 %. Similarly, Li+ rejection was also relatively high (28.25 %) at this pH, as the membrane repelled monovalent Li+ ions more due to its strong positive charge. As the pH increased toward neutral values, both amine and carboxyl groups began to deprotonate, reducing the mem­brane’s net positive charge.

The A2-G0.3 membrane exhibited low rejections of 20.6 %, 20.8 %, and 19.9 % for monovalent salts, including LiCl, NaCl, and KCl, respectively. In contrast, it showed significantly higher rejections for salts with divalent cations, achieving 88.0 % for MgCl2 and 71.4 % for CaCl2. According to the Dannan exclusion theory, a membrane with a more positive charge surface shows a stronger rejection for divalent cations like Mg2+ and Ca2+ than monovalent cations like Li+, Na+, and K+ [45]. Additionally, the smaller hydrated diameter and lower dehydration energy of monovalent cations would facilitate their passage through the membrane. Interestingly, the A2-G0.3 membrane exhibited higher rejection for MgCl2 (88.0 %) compared to CaCl2 (71.4 %), attributed to the larger hydrated diameter and dehydration energy of Mg2+ compared to Ca2+ [9]. Furthermore, the membrane showed high rejections for salts containing divalent an­ions, with 85.3 % for Na2SO4 and 91.3 % for MgSO4. The lower rejection of MgCl2 compared to Na2SO4 and MgSO4 can be attributed to the smaller hydrated diameter of Cl-(0.648 nm) versus SO42-(0.758 nm), as well as the higher energy barrier (-1145 kJ mol-1) required for SO42-to shed its hydration shell, compared to Cl-(-363 kJ mol-1).

The performance of the optimized A2-G0.3 membrane in this study is compared with recent works on NF membranes for Li+/Mg2+ separation (Table 3). Many of these NF membranes rely on amine-containing monomers, which are either expensive to synthesize or purchase, and their fabrication processes are often complex. Additionally, while some of these membranes demonstrate high Mg2+ rejection, they also exhibit high Li+ rejection, which is undesirable for effective lithium recovery. Another critical challenge is the trade-off between water permeability and Li+/Mg2+ selectivity, as higher selectivity often comes at the cost of reduced water flux, limiting the membranes’ practical applications.

Stability concerns, such as leaching of incorporated additives (e.g., metal ions, ionic liquids, or crown ethers), further complicate long-term operation and scale-up. In contrast, the A2-G0.3 membrane developed in this study overcomes these challenges by utilizing cost-effective and widely available materials, achieving both high Li+/Mg2+ selectivity and high water permeability. The membrane also demonstrates antifouling properties and is suitable for commercial scale-up, making it a promising candidate for lithium recovery from saline water sources. Finally, the performance of the optimized membrane (A2-G0.3) was further evaluated with two distinct simulated brines to assess its po­tential for practical applications. Simulated brine I included Li+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, and Ca2+ cations with a total concentration exceeding 20,000 ppm, as specified in Table 4. The weight ratios of Li+ to other cations were based on the compositions of Clayton Valley and Smackover brines in the United States [59,60]. As illustrated in Fig. 9a, the membrane effectively rejected divalent cations, achieving 90.1 % rejection for Mg2+ and 75.4 % for Ca2+ while allowing the passage of monovalent cations with rejections of 9.5 % for Li+, 12.8 % for Na+, and 12.4 % for K+.

The water flux of the membrane was also reduced slightly from 3.85 Lm-2h-1.bar-1 to 3.44 Lm-2h-1bar-1 after 24 h due to increased osmotic pressure. A comparison of membrane performance in the two brines revealed a higher Li+/Mg2+ selectivity in brine II compared to brine I. In brine I, the membrane exhibited a Li+/Mg2+ selectivity of 9.14 after 1 h of filtration and 5.15 after 24 h. In contrast, brine II showed a higher selectivity, with values of 12.56 after 1 h and 9.19 after 24 h. The Li+/Mg2+ lower selectivity in brine I can be attributed to the presence of other mono­valent cations, such as Na+ and K+, which competed with Li+ for permeation through the membrane, aiming to maintain the neutrality of the permeate. Conclusion This research developed modified poly(PIP-amide) NF membranes through IP reaction between PIP and TMC, followed by surface modi­fication with ARG and crosslinking with GLA for efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation. Comprehensive characterization, including FTIR, XPS, AFM, SEM, and TEM, confirmed the successful formation of an ARG-GLA grafting layer on the membrane surface.

Zeta potential analysis revealed a significant improvement in positive surface charge for the ARG-modified membranes compared to the non-modified TFC mem­branes. Specifically, the optimized A2-G0.3 sample exhibited an iso­electric point of 5.5, compared to 2.9 for the P0.5 membrane. Moreover, surface modification with ARG and GLA improved the membrane’s wettability, as evidenced by a lower WCA of 34.1◦± 3.1 for the A2-G0.3 membrane, compared to 43.9◦± 2.6 for the P0.5 membrane. The A2G0.3 sample also achieved a Li+/Mg2+ of 17.11, significantly higher than the 5.14 obtained for the P0.5 sample. Specifically, the A2-G0.3 sample achieved a Mg2+ rejection of 91.5 %, a Li+ rejection of -45.4 %, and a water flux of 47.0 Lm-2h-1 when tested in a solution with a total concentration of 2000 ppm (Li+/Mg2+ of 1:20). Furthermore, grafting the ARG-GLA layer onto the membrane surface significantly enhanced its antifouling performance. The A2-G0.3 membrane demon­strated enhanced antifouling performance compared to the P0.5 mem­brane across all three fouling solutions: SA with CaCl2, HA, and BSA. Additionally, the A2-G0.3 membrane was tested in two distinct simu­lated brines with concentrations exceeding 20,000 ppm, demonstrating efficient Li+/Mg2+ separation.

Overall, the A2-G0.3 membrane dem­onstrates significant potential for commercial-scale lithium recovery from brines due to its straightforward and cost-effective fabrication process. Unlike other NF membranes that rely on expensive aminecontaining monomers or complex synthesis steps, the A2-G0.3 mem­brane is fabricated using widely available and affordable materials. The IP process used in its fabrication is already well-established in mem­brane manufacturing, making large-scale production feasible without requiring significant modifications to current industrial processes. Un­like many existing membranes, which suffer from a trade-off between permeability and selectivity, the A2-G0.3 membrane maintains both high separation performance and water flux. This balance reduces en­ergy consumption in large-scale operations, further improving its com­mercial viability. Additionally, the enhanced antifouling properties suggest that the membrane can maintain long-term operational stability with minimal cleaning requirements. This characteristic is crucial for industrial applications, where membrane fouling can increase opera­tional costs and downtime.

Given these advantages, the poly(PIP-amide) membrane modified with ARG and GLA presents a promising and scal­able solution for lithium extraction from saline water sources, aligning with the growing demand for sustainable and cost-efficient lithium re­covery technologies.
